MCEN2002 · FUNDAMENTALS OF MECHANICAL DESIGN
Chapter 1 Properties of Plane Area

First Moment of Area & Centroid

The first moment of area \(\left(\int y\, dA\right)\) tells you where a cross-section balances — its centroid. This is the reference point for every bending stress calculation that follows.
Section 1.1 First Moment & Centroid Section 1.2 Second Moment of Area Section 1.3 Parallel Axis Theorem Section 1.4 Polar Moment Section 1.5 Summary
Before we begin

Why does shape matter?

Hold a flat ruler by its ends. Bend it the easy way — it flexes immediately. Now rotate it 90° and try to bend it the hard way — it resists far more. The material hasn't changed. Only the shape has.

This is the central insight of Chapter 1. The resistance a beam, shaft, or structural member offers to bending and twisting is not just about what it's made of — it depends critically on the geometry of its cross-section. Engineers deliberately choose I-beams, hollow tubes, and T-sections not because they look good, but because the distribution of material around an axis determines how effectively that material is used.

🎯 Where you will use this

The three quantities you learn in this chapter — centroid, second moment of area \(I\), and polar moment of area \(J\) — appear directly in the stress equations for bending beams (Chapter 3), shafts under torsion (Chapter 4), and column buckling (Chapter 6). Every structural calculation in this course depends on getting these right first.

INTERACTIVE — DRAG TO ROTATE · SCROLL TO ZOOM
Solid rectangular bar

Most material sits near the neutral axis

Steel I-beam (girder)

Same area — material placed far from neutral axis

Both beams have identical cross-sectional area · Gold line = neutral axis

The underside of a steel bridge — every horizontal member you see is an I-beam or box girder. The cross-sectional shape is chosen to maximise the second moment of area for the available material. (image from unsplash.com)
Concept 1 of 4

The Centroid

🎯 Why this concept

Before you can calculate how a section resists bending, you must know where to measure from. The centroid defines the reference point — the geometric "balance point" of a cross-section — from which all moment calculations are made. Getting the centroid wrong means every downstream calculation is wrong.

Intuition first: the balance point

Imagine cutting a flat plate into any arbitrary shape from cardboard. If you were to balance it on the tip of a pencil, there is exactly one point where it would sit level. That point is the centroid — the geometric centre of the area. For symmetric shapes, it sits on the axis of symmetry. For asymmetric shapes, it requires calculation.

The board balances at exactly one point — its centroid. Move the finger slightly and it tips. This is what the centroid formula finds: the geometric balance point of any cross-section.
The ⊕ symbol marks the centroid of each shape. For the rectangle and circle, it falls at the geometric centre — intuitive and expected. For the L-shape, the centroid falls outside the solid material entirely — in the empty corner. This may surprise you — and is exactly why asymmetric sections always require calculation.

The centroid of a composite area

Real engineering cross-sections are rarely simple shapes. They are usually composite areas — combinations of rectangles, circles, and triangles. The centroid of the composite is found by a weighted average: shapes with more area pull the centroid toward them.

Centroid Location — Composite Area
\[\bar{x} = \frac{\sum A_i \, \bar{x}_i}{\sum A_i} \qquad \bar{y} = \frac{\sum A_i \, \bar{y}_i}{\sum A_i}\]
\(\bar{x}, \bar{y}\) Centroid coordinates of the composite section (mm or m)
\(A_i\) Area of each sub-component (mm² or m²)
\(\bar{x}_i, \bar{y}_i\) Centroid coordinates of each sub-component measured from a reference origin
\(\Sigma\) Sum over all sub-components

Important note on holes: A hole (cutout) is treated as a sub-component with negative area. This is the standard method — subtract it from the whole.

EXAMPLE 01 Centroid of a T-Section (Two Rectangles)

Given: A T-section made of two rectangles with the dimensions below. Find the centroid \(\bar{y}\) from the bottom of the section.

Part \(A_i\) (mm²) \(\bar{y}_i\) from base (mm) \(A_i \bar{y}_i\) (mm³)
\(R_1\) (flange) 160 × 50 = 8 000 140 + 50/2 = 165 1 320 000
\(R_2\) (web) 50 × 140 = 7 000 140/2 = 70 490 000
\(\Sigma\) 15 000 — 1 810 000
RESULT
\[\begin{aligned} \bar{y} &= \frac{A_1 \bar{y}_1 + A_2 \bar{y}_2}{A_1 + A_2} \\[10pt] &= \frac{(8000)(165) + (7000)(70)}{8000 + 7000} \\[10pt] &= \frac{1{,}810{,}000}{15{,}000} = \textbf{120.7 mm} \end{aligned}\]
from the base
EXAMPLE 02 Centroid of a Rectangle with a Circular Hole

Given: A 200 × 150 mm rectangle with a circular hole of diameter 60 mm centred at (100, 75) mm. Find the centroid. Note: the hole is treated as a negative area.

Part \(A_i\) (mm²) \(\bar{x}_i\) (mm) \(\bar{y}_i\) (mm)
Rectangle +30 000 100 75
Circle (hole) −2 827 100 75
\(\Sigma\) 27 173 — —
RESULT
\[\begin{aligned} \bar{x} &= \frac{A_1 \bar{x}_1 + A_2 \bar{x}_2}{A_1 + A_2} \\[10pt] &= \frac{(30000)(100) + (-2827)(100)}{30000 + (-2827)} \\[10pt] &= \frac{2{,}717{,}300}{27{,}173} = \textbf{100 mm} \end{aligned}\]
\[\begin{aligned} \bar{y} &= \frac{A_1 \bar{y}_1 + A_2 \bar{y}_2}{A_1 + A_2} \\[10pt] &= \frac{(30000)(75) + (-2827)(75)}{30000 + (-2827)} \\[10pt] &= \frac{2{,}037{,}975}{27{,}173} = \textbf{75 mm} \end{aligned}\]

Because both the rectangle and the hole share the same centroid coordinates, the composite centroid stays at the geometric centre. Try moving the hole off-centre — the centroid shifts.

💡

Bridge to Section 1.2
Coming Up Next

You can now find the centroid of any composite section, no matter how the pieces are arranged. The next question is how that same section resists bending — that's governed by the Second Moment of Area, \(I\), which you'll compute about the centroidal axis you just learned to locate.
